title: America Needs More Famous Families | Institute for Family Studies
gist: Survey data show the family sizes of admired celebrities correlate with their fans' own desired family sizes, suggesting celebrity culture is a lever on fertility: fans of childless icons want fewer kids, fans of big-family stars want more.

insights.claim: Fans who named childless figures Pope Leo XIV and Billie Eilish as most admired reported some of the lowest desired family sizes (2.04 and 2.1 children).
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Suggests admiration for high-status childless icons is associated with lower fertility aspirations.

insights.claim: Superfans of high-parity celebrities Cardi B (4 kids), Michael Jordan (5), Tom Brady (3) and Chris Brown (3) all reported wanting 3.2 or more children, topping the desired-family-size ranking.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Admiring big-family celebrities tracks with meaningfully higher fertility desire — a cultural, not just economic, fertility lever.
insights.locator: At the opposite end of the spectrum

insights.claim: The piece implies enlisting famous families as role models could raise fertility, arguing 'America needs more famous families.'
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Frames pronatal cultural intervention (celebrity influence) as a policy and campaign opportunity distinct from cash incentives.
